ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class (FOODON_00002473); no specific tofu-dashi udon entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers cooked udon noodles as a base; no composite tofu-dashi udon dish code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Japanese beancurd udon with dashi, approximately 450 g, comprising udon noodles in dashi broth with silken tofu, as typically served at a Japanese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle soup with tofu — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Udon noodles cooked (FDC ID 168917), Silken tofu (FDC ID 172475), and Dashi broth (FDC ID 169238); proportions estimated from standard Japanese udon-tofu soup recipe (approx. 45% broth, 35% noodles, 15% tofu, 5% condiments)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Japanese beancurd udon with dashi as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked udon noodles, silken tofu, and dashi broth using standard recipe proportions. Energy calculated by Atwater factors. Sodium elevated due to dashi and soy sauce seasoning.

gi_source.title: Udon noodles, white, boiled — GI value 55 (glucose reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Japanese beancurd udon with dashi as a composite dish. GI of 55 applied from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white udon noodles, boiled (GI=55, glucose reference), which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor. Silken tofu and dashi broth contribute negligible additional glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 51.75) / 100 ≈ 28.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 11.5 × 4.5 = 51.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium seasoning, as a single 450 g bowl provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined udon noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving provides approximately 52 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~28.5; portion control and pairing with additional protein or fibre-rich vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate carbohydrate load; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1395 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at approximately 0.9 g per serving, and the dish contains heart-neutral soy protein from tofu, making fat profile acceptable but sodium management critical for cardiovascular patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Beancurd Udon with Dashi is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles served in a clear, umami-rich dashi broth, topped with soft silken tofu (beancurd). Dashi is a traditional Japanese stock typically made from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes), providing a delicate savoury depth. The silken tofu adds a smooth, custard-like texture and contributes plant-based protein. The dish is typically seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, and garnished with sliced spring onions and nori. As served at GyoGyo (Funan, Singapore), it is a restaurant-prepared Japanese comfort food. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides a moderate carbohydrate load primarily from the udon noodles. Sodium content is notably high due to the dashi broth and soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of udon noodles is approximately 55 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~28.5). The tofu component contributes calcium, iron, and soy isoflavones. This dish is suitable for dairy-free diets but contains wheat (gluten) and soy allergens, and the dashi broth typically contains fish-derived ingredients, making it unsuitable for vegetarians and vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as served. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked udon noodles, silken tofu, and dashi broth using standard recipe proportions. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white udon noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component source entries used for this composite dish estimate. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats expected in this minimally processed dish based on USDA component data). The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu label '608D Japanese Beancurd Udon With Dashi B' to the canonical English name 'Japanese Beancurd Udon with Dashi'; the original menu label is preserved in aliases. Sodium is notably high due to dashi broth and soy sauce; exact sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. GI category is low (GI=55, which is ≤55 per Veritas threshold rules).
